Urges States to fulfil their commitment to ensure that safe, effective and of guaranteed quality medicines, including generic medicines, vaccines, diagnostics and other health technologies, to prevent, diagnose and treat HIV infection, its co-infections and co-morbidities, are accessible, available and affordable globally, by urgently removing, as far as possible, all obstacles, including barriers to regulations, policies and practices that impede access to technology and to the achievement of health goals, and by promoting the use of all available tools to reduce the prices of health technologies and the costs associated with the lifelong management of chronic conditions, and to promote a fair and equitable distribution of health products among countries and countries, in order to advance the full realization of the right to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health;

31. Calls upon the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS and its sponsoring organizations to assist countries in addressing the legal, social, economic, political and structural factors of the AIDS epidemic, including through the promotion of all human rights, gender equality and the empowerment of all women and girls;

32. Urges States to create and maintain a safe and enabling environment, online and offline, in which civil society, including communities of people living with HIV, who are at risk of being infected or affected by the virus, including women, adolescents and youth, persons with disabilities and key populations, can operate unhindered, safely and without reprisals, including through the development of relevant laws, policies, institutions and mechanisms and, where necessary, the review and modification of existing ones, and also urges States to ensure that such measures take into account gender, disability and age issues, address stigma associated with HIV and AIDS and take into account the needs of different groups, including key populations, and that threats and attacks also occur online;

33. Urges urgent action to address the lack of HIV and AIDS resources, taking into account the need for an additional annual investment of $8 billion and increased investment in societal levers, in order to achieve the 2025 targets on the basis of shared responsibility and global solidarity, calls upon countries to increase national and international funding for the fight against HIV, and stresses the need for action to ensure political, programmatic and financial accountability and sustainable and equitable funding at all levels;

34. Urges States to take measures to sustain progress in the fight against HIV beyond 2030 and to respect, protect and realize the human rights of people living with HIV, at risk of infection or affected by the virus, in order to reduce vulnerability to HIV and improve access to services, inter alia, but not exclusively, by eliminating HIV-related stigma and discrimination, eliminating discriminatory laws, establishing gender equality and protecting civic space;

35. Requests the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, in consultation with the secretariat of the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS and relevant experts, to organize, at its fifty-eighth session, a round table open to persons with disabilities and open to participation by States, local authorities, treaty bodies and relevant special procedures, academia, civil society and other stakeholders, in order to consider the question of the realization of human rights in the context of the sustainability and strengthening of progress in the response to HIV and the need to leave no one behind, and to make recommendations to countries in this regard;

36. Also requests the High Commissioner to prepare, under agenda item 3, a report on a sustainable response to HIV with regard to the human rights of people living with, at risk of infection or affected by HIV, taking into account the outcome of the round table to be held at the fifty-eighth session, and to submit the report to the General Assembly at its sixtieth session in forms accessible to persons with disabilities;

37. Further requests the High Commissioner to prepare a report, in consultation with Governments, civil society, community-led organizations and other stakeholders, on the impact, results and status of the implementation of societal levers by States, as recognized in the Political Declaration on HIV and AIDS: ending inequalities and addressing AIDS by 2030, and to submit it to the General Assembly at its sixty-fourth session, in forms accessible to persons with disabilities, and to communicate its conclusions and recommendations to the Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS, the World Health Organization, the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria and other stakeholders, as a contribution to its work to assist States in sustaining and strengthening progress in the response to HIV and leaving no one behind;

38. Requests the High Commissioner to invite States and all other stakeholders, including relevant United Nations bodies, agencies, funds and programmes, special procedures, in particular the Special Rapporteur on the right of everyone to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health, treaty bodies, regional human rights and health organizations and bodies, national human rights institutions and civil society, including persons living or presumed to be living with HIV, who are at risk of infection or who are affected by the virus, to contribute to the reports.
